sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Anaphylaxis is a severe, rapidly developing systemic allergic reaction that can cause airway compromise, bronchospasm, and shock. In prehospital care, intramuscular adrenaline is the first-line treatment and should not be delayed when anaphylaxis is suspected.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Acute onset illness with airway swelling, hoarse voice, stridor, wheeze, persistent cough, or difficult breathing
- Hypotension, collapse, or signs of shock
- Skin features such as urticaria, flushing, or angioedema may be present but are not required for diagnosis
- Gastrointestinal symptoms can occur, especially with insect or medication allergy
- Reduced conscious state may reflect hypoxia or shock

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Keep the patient lying flat or in another position they can tolerate safely; avoid standing or unnecessary mobilisation
- Prepare early for airway deterioration
- Multiple doses of IM adrenaline may be required
- Patients require transport and observation after treatment because deterioration or biphasic reactions can occur
- Nebulised bronchodilator therapy is an adjunct for wheeze and does not replace adrenaline

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Remove the trigger if this can be done safely
- Administer IM adrenaline immediately or use an adrenaline autoinjector if appropriate and available
- Position the patient appropriately, prioritising circulatory safety and airway protection
- Give oxygen if clinically indicated
- Monitor pulse, blood pressure, respiratory status, and conscious state
- Arrange urgent transport for all cases

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Repeat IM adrenaline when the response is inadequate or symptoms recur, according to current guidance and local protocol
- Establish IV or IO access and give fluid boluses for shock when indicated
- Consider inhaled bronchodilator therapy for persistent lower-airway bronchospasm
- IV adrenaline infusion should only be used within protocol or with senior critical-care guidance when refractory anaphylaxis persists
- Escalate urgently for airway obstruction, persistent hypotension, or peri-arrest state

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Antihistamines and corticosteroids are not first-line treatment for life-threatening anaphylaxis
- Current ASCIA guidance should be followed for dose, timing, and post-treatment observation principles
